content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Films where love is not enough

**Score:** 32
**Body:** I'm looking for a film about two people who love and respect each other, but break up due to incompability, values, timing or wanting different things. Nothing toooo devastating, ideally! Ty in advance ​

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Casablanca 
  - *We’ll always have Paris…* 
2. Roman Holiday - some say the best love story ever. It’s a classic for a reason. 
3. Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

Idk if it breaks the rule of too devastating. I think it's a fantastic film, and I definitely cried lol. Highly recommend it though!
4. La La Land
5. Blue Valentine
6. The Remains of the Day
7. Past Lives (2023) is really good! 
8. brokeback mountain, 500 days of summer
  - The first time I saw Brokeback Mountain was shortly after it released. As a straight man from the southern United States. I was surprised how moved i was by 2 closeted cowboys just wanting to be themselves. It was a shock at the time and actually made me love and respect the LGBTQ+ community more for what they had to deal with at those times.

## 2. Movie suggestions for bed ridden grandpa

**Score:** 29
**Body:** My grandpa is a polio survivor and had to get the hardware in his knee taken out. He’s been bed-ridden/couch ridden for about the last month. I got a heartbreaking phone call from him saying he’s bored and kinda making him depressed. I’m traveling down to see him and bring him a care package with some movies but need some help. He LOVES John Wayne movies and the Tom Selleck westerns. 

However, I was wanting to come up with a few movies he hasn’t seen 100 times so maybe some more modern western or western adjacent suggestions?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. True Grit! You could watch the old one and the new one and compare them. 
2. Tombstone 
3. Willy Wonka. The ultimate grandpa stuck in bed story lmao 🤣
4. A River Runs Through It
5. Not a western, but The Great Escape.  It's a great movie with a fantastic cast, and it will kill 3 hours.
6. Blazing Saddles - he could use a good laugh. 
7. Rio Bravo or El Dorado
8. Depends how tolerant he is of sex, violence & swearing but Deadwood might be worth a shot.
9. Not a Movie, but my grandma **LOVE** Blue Bloods with Tom Selleck - the show was on for 14 years so there should be plenty to watch before getting bored.
10. Has he seen Tremors?

## 3. Suggest me many dumb fun movies

**Score:** 30
**Body:** As the title says, please suggest some good dumb fun movies to watch. I don't care what it's rating on rotten tomatoes or imdb, or etc. I need a good laugh to end this bored nights of mine. I love movies like The Dictator, Hangover Series, Eurotrip, etc.

Thank you!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Hot Rod….”Cool beans”
2. Team America: World Police
3. Anything by Mel Brooks

Free Guy

The Fall Guy 

Argylle 

Clue

4. Tropic thunder
5. hot tub time machine
6. Top Secret

Super Troopers

Don't Be a Menace to South Central While Drinking Your Juice in the Hood

Black Dynamite 
7. White chicks.
8. Three O’Clock High
9. Dumb & Dumber

Romy and Michelle's High School Reunion

Zoolander

The Waterboy

Old School
10. Shaun of the Dead

## 4. Movies that cover several genres, like The Gorge

**Score:** 26
**Body:** I just watched The Gorge and loved it, I felt like it had everything! there was Romance, comedy, horror, sci fi, and action! I'm looking for other movies that fit several genres, especially these 5. The only other movie I can think of that had these was Super 8 (which I quite liked too). 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Everything,everywhere all at once.
2. Top of my head suggestion for all those genres is “The Rocky Horror Picture Show” but you’d be forgiven if it’s not what you’re looking for. Well worth the watch if you haven’t seen it

Maybe “Re-Animator” & “From Beyond” as a fun (science fiction 😉) double feature
3. You'll probably like **Edge of Tomorrow** then
4. Armageddon 
5. Bone Tomahawk (2015)

Bones and All (2022)
6. Dale And Tucker Vs Evil

Comedy/ horror
7. **Hot Fuzz** - it even throws in some southern gothic-ish elements.

**John dies at the end**


8. I’d argue Sinners and Weapons from just this past year
9. Warm Bodies 

Death of a Unicorn

Cowboys and Aliens

Pride and Prejudice and Zombies
10. The Host (2026) Korean monster flick with comedy and family drama

## 5. Movies like The Endless (2017)?

**Score:** 17
**Body:** suggest me cosmic horror movies like The Endless (2017) where the "creature" or "entity" is literally unknown and humans cannot see them or imagine what it would look like. 

I also found that Bird Box (2018) portrays this as well. The creature is never shown on screen because we humans would not even be able to comprehend what "it" is.

The Thing (1982) may or may not be a good example of this. We CAN indeed see and comprehend the shape-shifting creature but we don't know what it actually is. Well it might be an alien because the beginning of the movie shows a UFO. 

The Endless and Bird Box are 2 movies that i found portrays this cosmic horror genre the best

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you didn’t know, The Endless is a follow up to the directors’ first movie, Resolution. It’s not a direct sequel but both movies are connected. It’s more intimate(only 2 characters)than The Endless but also more horror than sci-fi.
  - excellent movies if you piece them all together on your own. i think there are 4 in total that have hints towards each other
2. You are looking for Lovecraftian Horror!


Underwater 2020

The color out of space 2019 (and if you like this one, Mandy 2018)

Event Horizon 1997

Annihilation 2018

The Ritual 2017 (super fkin scary)

The cabin in the woods (2011)

The mist 2007
3. The Void

In the Mouth of Madness

Annihilation

Pandorum

Event Horizon

Sunshine

Prometheus

Color Out of Space

Gehenna: Where Death Lives

Triangle
4. The Ritual (2017). Absolutely love this film. I enjoyed the book but I think there are aspects added to the movie that give it a much clearer through line to string the theme together 

The Empty Man (2020). Reviews swear up and down that the 20 minute cold open is the only good part of the movie. To some degree, yes it's more potent than the remainder of the movie, but I think the rest of the movie has merit. Some really interesting ideas and sequences but some may find the more vague aspects to be unsatisfying. Not groundbreaking but should've gotten more attention. This one is based off a short graphic novel worth picking up
5. I have to watch this now finally. Wanted a cosmic horror last week and watched The Empty Man instead if this, it was good. 
6. Color Out of Space
7. Something in the Dirt (2022) is also by Benson and Moorhead. 

It’s a different concept than The Endless, but it should scratch that itch. 
8. The Forgotten (2004) — fits the best, i think 

Bokeh (was a bit slow for me but everyone on earth except for the 2 MCs suddenly disappear with no explanation)

The Night House — entity is invisible, and u known until the very end. 

Spontaneous — completely different vibe since it’s a horror comedy, but great script (very witty) and it’s never explained why teenagers are blowing up afaik 

The Banshee Chapter, kind of. We do see them but only quick flashes and we don’t know exactly what they are, only that they “came through”

Come True, kind of. We see them but it’s kind of vague/dreamy
9. The Void 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Are you a fan?

**Score:** 916
**Body:** Gangs of New York (2002) Excellent gritty movie about gangs in the five points of New York in the 1800s. Excellent performances and great writing, so are you a fan?

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes, a personal favorite. Just look at this set. 

https://preview.redd.it/7rak0s99txug1.png?width=2560&format=png&auto=webp&s=875aab01c40e7d3c9dc5ca85aabd6a23fa045125


  - Movie making can be so cool when people want it to be
  - George Lucas apparently visited while filming and told Scorsese “You know they can do stuff like this with computers now”
2. Honestly Cameron Diaz's performance is the only thing I don't like about this film. Bill the Butcher is one of my favorite movie villains of all time. "You see this knife? I'm gonna teach you to speak English with this knife."
  - I fully agree with this sentiment 
  - Yeh I kind of have to agree with this. I’d rewatched it recently, & in hindsight she didn’t gel well in the film. 

Love the opening sequence with Liam Neeson, & Brendan Gleeson is brilliant regardless of his minimal time on screen.
3. The time lapse scene at the end was absolutely brilliant.   
4. Weinstein reportedly made Scorsese cut out an hour because of which I have always want to see the longer edit. (I know the release cut is very long) I feel there is something missing in Amsterdam’s character development and I don’t think it’s Leo’s performance. Diaz is miscast but gets too much hate. I don’t think she does the movie and real damage. 

Those criticisms aside, I love this movie a lot because I am an historian of the 19th century and although it takes some liberties with the Five Points timeline and overall chronology, it gets so much right. Production value is tops from period costume and Five Points layout and conditions, street slang, and mentalities. 

DDL is the cold violent heart of the movie and he’s fantastic. I also love the fathers theme. 
5. daniel day-lewis as bill the butcher is one of the most terrifying villains ever, literally had chills during that knife scene.
  - https://preview.redd.it/tfvpa5potxug1.jpeg?width=350&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=5e8ce380d7c03b2f5e1eab01c69a82aa40ffee5f

He was definitely terrifying such a great performance Imo was his best

## 2. All Quiet on the Western front: War doesn’t create heroes; it creates apologies that come too late.

**Score:** 784
**Body:** From All Quiet on the Western Front; a moment that hits harder than any battlefield scene.

A teenager sent to fight a war he never chose… forced to kill a man he never hated.

And in the end, all that’s left is guilt, a photograph, and a broken apology.

We glorify war in stories, medals, and history books 

but scenes like this remind us:

there are no winners, only survivors and regrets.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes indeed. Well summarized. 
2. Such a brutal scene and movie. 
3. The end is fantastic. It shows how one person with authority, with delusions of a glorious last fight,  can cause many to die for absolutely nothing. 
  - It's one of those things that makes you wonder why they didn't just kill the officer. 
  - I'm sorry but I thought the end was absolutely terrible. 

1) that never happened. The Germans didn't pull any last minute stunts like that at wars close.

2) This ending was a slap on the face to the original book and missed the entire point of the story. 
    - I read the book after the movie and yeah, the ending in the book hits a lot harder. Granted, I think you couldn't do it like the book since it is intentionally a very unceremonious and anti climactic end for Paul, but they could've done it better. Just remove the final charge after the end of the war and have it still set during the war and make the death less dramatic. Keep the scene with the fresh young soldier collecting the dog tag because that's genuinely a very good scene to end on.

In general, I feel like the side plot of the evil general and the peace negotiations shouldn't even be part of the movie.
  - I felt the ending was kind of bolted on: a comical bad guy, ignoring reason, sending good troops after bad?

We don't need that to learn that war is senseless and people die for no reason. We have basically everything that happened on the Western Front from Sept 1915 to roughly Sept 1918 to tell us that.

From a historical perspective, nothing like that ever happened, and it's totally absurd even in a movie where everything is absurd.
4. They must have changed a lot for the remake because in the book and 30s movie they were jumping to go to war & it’s very clear how on-board they were (and most of the entire country was) with going to war, so you could not really say it was something they did not choose it was more “here’s what you asked for, here’s what it’s actually like, do you still feel proud for the chance to die for your country”
  - The kids are still very gung-ho about going to war in this remake, but it’s because they’ve had propaganda shoved down their throats. They had an idea of war sold to them, but the reality of war was a much different thing. 
5. The scene is heartbreaking.

However, both the movie and especially the book made it very clear that Paul actively chose to join the war, not only voluntarily, but his friends even forged their parent‘s signature to join the army.

## 3. Movies with three outstanding performances?

**Score:** 230
**Body:** Film - No Country for Old Men (2007)

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fargo had a well deserved Oscar win for Francis McDormand, a well deserves Oscar nomination for William H Macy and an absolutely chilling performance from Peter Stromare. 
  - And everyone. buschemi n her fellow cops, her husband, his wife n her dad, Proud foot, the escort.
2. Sicario, also Brolin 

Banshees of Inisherin?

Weapons? (Brolin as well, hmmm)

Seven Psychopaths 
  - Seven Psychopaths is a great movie. 
3. Dr. Strangelove, all by the same guy!
4. Forrest Gump.  The departed.  Goodfellas 
5. The good the bad and the ugly. Unforgiven. 
6. Logjammin 
7. The big lebowski 
8. The Sting 

Robert Redford 

Paul Newman 

Robert Shaw 

## 4. When you see this woman what movie/tv show comes to mind......?

**Score:** 156

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It should be Misery, but it's The Waterboy. She was so freaking hilarious. 
  - Foosball is the devil
2. The unsinkable Molly Brown from Titanic.
3. Honestly? Fried Green Tomatoes.
  - Face it, I'm older and I have better insurance!
  - **TOWANDA!!!**
4. Misery! Not my Misery!
5. Back rewatching American Horror Story: Coven. She plays a great part 
6. The Office
7. That’s just Bobby’s momma 

## 5. The Drop (2014) - slow burn movies with a shock ending yet still worthy of rewatches?

**Score:** 135
**Body:** Threw this on again last night and even though I know the ending and it's all a build up to the climax scene it's just such a tight ride. RIP James.

What did you think of it, and what other titles are similar?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. "No one ever sees you coming, do they Bob?"
  - Yeah, this is such an underrated film. Definitely in my top 100. 
2. If you liked this one, then I recommend you "Killing Them Softly", which also has Gandolfini in it 😉 
  - I have yet to find a bad movie with James Gandolfini in it
3. Great short story too. Called Animal Rescue by Dennis Lehane.

Edit
4. Banger of a flick!  Both one of Gandolfini's and Hardy's best roles too.

Oh damn:  totally forgot that Naomi rapace was in it too.
5. I watched it last week. Amazing movie.
6. “You wouldn’t call someone from Ireland Irelandian, would you?” 
7. Great Movie I watched it again recently 
8. Hardy reminded me of Brando in this movie. He has always had *that* quality but here it was very noticeable.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Has a film ever made you think about the choices you make or the way you think?

**Score:** 81
**Body:** Not just something you liked, but something that made you think about your own life or choices. Even if they aren't perfect, those are the films that stick with me the longest. I think it's interesting how a film can change how we think or feel over time. Different people connect to different stories based on their own experiences. For instance, films like Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind and Requiem for a Dream seem to have that effect by focusing on how characters think and feel instead of just the story. I am especially interested in how films do this through their stories, character development, or tone, and not just by being shocking or intense.  
Which films have had that kind of effect on you, and what about them made them hit you so hard? Did they change the way you see things?

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Oh yes, *Harvey*. Just about every day I think about Jimmy Stewart’s line when asked why he doesn’t have any “righteous indignation”:

> Years ago my mother used to say to me, she'd say, "In this world, Elwood, you must be" - she always called me Elwood - "In this world, Elwood, you must be oh so smart or oh so pleasant." Well, for years I was smart. I recommend pleasant. You may quote me.

It’s not always easy, but if you can catch yourself on the verge of being smart and instead be pleasant it always, in my experience, works out better.
2. Uncut Gems by Safdie Brothers - as a someone who was once a compulsive gambler. I thought I was watching my whole life when I got hooked into gambling. The highs & the lows man. I did some bad things I'm not proud of and after watching that movie, the decisions I made back then, I don't even want to go back and relive the memories of the past. It just makes me hate myself. But I guess it's for the best because it motivates me to be better than I was and to never go back into that feeling again. 

Sometimes a film will impact you that much because it blends in with reality. 
3. Midsommar. I saw a lot of myself in Christian and that made me want to strive to be a better partner. Trying to listen more and actually hear what's been said instead of trying to downplay or devils advocate everything. .

The weird schism in the response to that movie is because Christian isn't *reeeaaally* shit. But he isn't all that great either.... A bit like Tottenham.
  - He’s pretty shit, let’s be honest.

But I also appreciate the self-reflection, and I do love when art can help us reflect and when it helps us admit not so great things about ourselves through it. 

For me these include Rose Byrne in if I Had Legs I’d Kick You who certainly makes a lot of bad choices but heck I know I’ve been there (and was there at the time I watched). And Everything Everywhere All At Once in Jobu and the edge between depression and meaningless and neurodivergency and queerness and third kid cultureness just hit so accurately like no movie before.
  - In real life, nothing he does would justify burning him to death but there are plenty of wish fulfillment films for men where they get to extravagantly punish their ex-girlfriend for being a bitch and it doesn't receive the same backlash. Suddenly women are too stupid to know that the film isn't real life.
4. I wouldn’t say it changed the way I think but After Life (1998) made me re-evaluate my entire life. It’s hard to even articulate because this movie hit me in deepest parts of my soul, but I’m slowly learning to live with more intention and gratitude because of some realizations I had while watching it
5. **Gone Baby Gone** made me do a deep introspective over principles vs consequentialism. When would I compromise my principals, how and why… It also helped me understand others doing so and really made me contemplate the effective purpose of our individually-imposed rules and what their purpose *should* be.
  - That's a great example, especially because it makes you think about principles and outcomes in a real-world way. I like how you framed it around rules that we make up ourselves because it makes me wonder if those rules really have a purpose or if they just give us a sense of order. Do you think the movie ends up challenging those ideas or supporting them more?
6. About Time made me stop trying to 'factory reset' / turn a new leaf on my life annually. For years I was trying to be perfect, I would give up on jobs, people, *friends* after the novelty wore off and I began to fear the sunk cost of letting things be.

In that film the protagonist kept rewinding time & orchestrating events until they were exactly how he wanted them. He lost years to cherry picking life & it was only after he met a road block in his power that stopped him from going back further did he begin to just accept things as they were & realise that things that way were actually *okay*.

It also made me revaluate some of the questionable actions of my father growing up that led to hardships in the family, the ending of that film really hit me hard emotionally made me want to try more and I've let go of a lot of the resentments for mistakes he made which affected my family. I spent a lot of years growing up wishing I had a different family, now I see that this is how things are and I should make hte most of it.
7. The House of Sand and Fog. I saw it as a teen and my (oversimplified) takeaway was “pay your bills on time.” Life is complex enough, so inviting another layer of chaos and desperation by neglecting your bills left an impression on me.

## 2. Arco is the anti-Blade Runner. The inverse of Ghost in the Shell. Have you seen it?

**Score:** 48
**Body:** I recently finished Arco, and was honestly blown away by this movie, as well as how *little* people seem to be talking about it. It definitely feels like it lives in the post-Blade Runner, post-Black Mirror, post-Ghost in the Shell universe.

That isn't to say that it's all magic-and-rainbows either (it isn't). It's just a movie with a lot more to say than: *the future is fucked.*

(Which is kind-of refreshing from a 2026 lens).

It also looks amazing, and is completely hand-drawn. It was created by a Parisian Illustrator who felt isolated and frustrated during Covid, and wanted to make a movie that wasn't steeped in dread.

I think you could either read the movie as a slice-of-life character study about two lost souls connecting in a darker, much larger world OR (and this is where it could feel like symbolic posturing) as an LGBT coming-of-age story.

Knowing the films origin, I don't *think* it was INTENDED as an LGBT coming-of-age story, but there is a lot of subtext leading to that. (Gender ambiguity being a reoccurring theme.)

I guess it depends on whether you believe a films meaning is owned by the Director, the entire production crew, the audience or all of us.

Regardless, if you're into Studio Ghibli and Moebius, you'll probably love this movie. It's immaculate looking, and I really loved tracing the look of the world to various films, and real-world locations.

I also have a video essay on it, if you want to hear more about its production, influences, themes and world: [https://youtu.be/EekRoNmArkQ?si=K3Xbd0bMQSDmh5PY](https://youtu.be/EekRoNmArkQ?si=K3Xbd0bMQSDmh5PY)

I really want to talk about this movie as I don't know anyone who has seen it. Have you seen it? Heard of it? What did you think?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Both Arco and Little Amélie were amazing french animated movies last year. I really loved both of them. 
Since we are talking about Arco, I really liked the bad guys >!them not being bad all the time at any cost, was really refreshing!< and the whole movie felt alive. Huge props to France (I'm Spanish so it's worth x5)
  - Ahh I really want to check out Little Amelie, thanks for reminding me. I heard it’s also really good. And yeah I thought the same thing about the bad guys. They’re antagonists but the real villain is.. well, nature, or real life issues like loneliness. It’s definitely more of an adult movie than a kids movie.
2. I was really invested about 30-40 minutes into the movie and convinced it was going to be amazing, but then the writing kinda fell flat for me and ventured into a childish, melodramatic anime type vibe where lots of emotional depth felt kinda forced/rushed and nearly funny in it's presentation. Particularly the part where the main character professes her love for Arco...like...I get that she feels alone and is 11 but hearing her say "I love you" at the end after spending just about 1 full day with this kid felt odd. Also that cooky group of three guys didn't end up playing a huge part in anything and their motivations suddenly aligned with sending Arco home extremely quickly after finally catching up with them. I also think the line "no one should know the future, ever" or whatever Arco mentioned had zero meaning? They ended up in some time-traveling chicken or egg scenario, but the result of arco's presence just made his future more likely. Not that I needed it to be some serious look at deterministic vs non-deterministic time traveling...but again the writing felt like it wanted to lead somewhere deeper and didn't.

All that being said I loved the art, loved the visual style, and the world-building. I would definitely recommend it, and those bits I took issue with, I'm able to look past because everything else was such good quality. I do think it was a really fun apocalyptic sci-fi idea.
3. Why do you say it’s LGBT? I was actually very refreshed to see that all the rainbows were more of a reference to the Noah’s Ark story. Note that they were saving samples of extinct plants and animals from a dying world. There is an ‘ark’ in the title.

And I say this as someone who saw the movie with a couple transgender people. They didn’t make any comments implying that.
4. I watched it in theaters earlier this year. I appreciated a lot about it, but I can't say I loved the entire thing. I loved the animation, loved the various character interactions, and the more positive outlook for the future. 


The reason I didn't love all of it was the pacing just felt off. Some of the conflicts get resolved too quickly. Some of the dialogue/actions felt a little out of nowhere or rushed. It happened enough times that I started to take a mental note every time I noticed. While it didn't ruin the movie, it felt like it could be extended in a few areas to really let the world breath a little. 


All that being said, the credit sequence was 10/10 for me. They did an amazing job at visual storytelling.
  - Really? Which conflicts in particular? I do kind of know what you mean, as I think the shorter runtime probably hampered the pacing in parts. The director is/was an animation teacher and literally hired his own students to make the movie so it was definitely working with a very indie budget, probably leading to a shorter runtime than other animated movies of the same league. Still, I’d say it deserves to be held up there with the giants like Ghibli, and is a pretty insane accomplishment. 
5. For sure worth checking out, really enjoyed it in my top 15 of last year. I thought it would pick up some steam when it got the Oscar’s nom but really didn’t. The inverse of the sci fi films you mentioned is a great reading didn’t come to mind for me although I was for sure enjoying a more hopeful look at the future.

Those flying scenes standout also loved the ending hit me way harder than anything I thought it had up its sleeve in that moment that you realize right when the character does (don’t want to spoil)
6. *Arco* is a beautiful movie. Its depiction of the future as some ongoing, societal healing process, it's certainly encouraging, although romantic and quite politically naive. Not a bad thing *per se*, btw.

What's really compelling about *Arco*, are both its aesthetic and symbolic subtleties. For example, the names of the main characters form the Spanish word for rainbow. But it's well-known how rare rainbows are, much like the exceptional, once in a lifetime encounter of Iris and Arco. The primary colors of the cartoon-like bad guys' clothes seem to coalesce in Arco's cape and its subsequent activation after they gave back the diamond. Iris hitting puberty as she fully engages with Arco as a significant other. The constant weeping of the characters, like some mourning in unison. And so on.

However, I fail to see in *Arco* what you're pointing at with the title of your post but also with your takes about 'LGBT coming-of-age-story,' post-anything. The way I see it, the movie tackles how the consequences of a world according to adults affect younger generations. Both Arco and Iris are spiritual rebels. Iris believe Arco's story due to her artistic sensibility. By the same token, Arco finds in Iris both a confident and a kindred soul. But this is so tragic, as their timelines are obviously disjointed. 

## 3. Project Hail Mary Chickens Out in the End

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Commander Yao tells Grace:

>Anyone can be brave. You just need to find someone to be brave for.

Ostensibly, then, the movie is about Grace finding that someone in Rocky and proving Commander Yao correct. Indeed, by the end, Grace is willing to give up his return home and risk his own safety to save his friend. Amaze. But the ending reveals that the movie doesn't really believe its own message.

If the movie really believed Grace was making a sacrifice, it would not have given us the final scene on Earth, because that is what Grace *and so the audience* are giving up. And if the movie really believed Grace was being brave, it would not have given us the final reunion with Rocky (speak nothing of the ending on Erid), because action in the face of uncertainty is at the heart of bravery, and neither Grace *nor the audience* needs to be brave if we know it's all going to work out in the end.

In *the Poetry Handbook*, John Lennard writes:

>Sonnets have served as love-poems and in courtship from Petrarch's fourteenth-century Italian, through Elizabethan sequences by Spenser, Sidney, and Shakespeare, to Elizabeth Barrett Browning's *Sonnets from the Portuguese* :  so when [Yeats used sonnet-form for Leda's brutal rape](https://www.poetryfoundation.org/poems/43292/leda-and-the-swan), he created tension between form and content, his choice of form in itself ironising his poem.

There is a similar tension between form and content here, but not for the better. Although Project Hail Mary speaks of sacrifice and bravery, it asks the audience to make no sacrifices and risk no unknowns. So, not a masterpiece. But it looks great, and Ryan Gosling is very charming!

TL;DR: The movie should have ended after Grace launched the probes.

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Your point alternates between bravery and sacrifice when the focus is mainly bravery and finding something to fight for.

Also the final scene takes place on Rocky’s home planet, not earth.
2. I loved The Martian and Project Hail Mary, but they're not overly complicated movies. They're telling simple stories with characters exhibiting essentially only positive traits. I think that's ok sometimes.


>because action in the face of uncertainty is at the heart of bravery


But he was uncertain about what would happen if he saved Rocky. At the very least, he knew that meant not going home (I'm not totally sure why, but that's the message in the film).
  - > he knew that meant not going home (I'm not totally sure why, but that's the message in the film).

Lack of fuel. In order to Save Rocky, he had to use the remaining fuel to travel in the wrong direction, away from Earth. 
    - I caught all of that. It just seemed like getting him back to Earth from Eridian should have been super easy, especially since they had the ability to produce astrophage at massive quantities. It's like the scene of Grace building a complex alien-translation system without having any background in that sort of thing. The characters are smart and so their ideas are good. That's how I try to view movies like this.
      - At the start the Eridians are in a similar position to the people of Earth, they too have never done interstellar space travel before. Worse, they messed it up because they didn't account for _relativity_ which they didn't know about. The justification is that without eyes, they didn't pay that much close attention to light. They're differently advanced, not super-advanced. it's a hugely expensive undertaking for either species. And it takes a few years.

It's no co-incidence that Grace is teaching speed of light at the end.

At that point they _do_ offer to return him to Earth as it has become possible, but he is not enthusiastic. 

My head-canon is that the 2 species would have continued to interact on good terms because of Grace and Rocky's example, i.e. they would have established communications within a few decades. Perhaps the Eridian were planning an expedition to Earth anyway, and were offering to take him along on it.
3. I don't know that the movie is fundamentally about "finding your bravery". I think that this premise is reductive.

The final scene is not on Earth.

Grace finally finds some purpose to his life: as a teacher in a context where he is highly valued for that, as he has perspectives and knowledge that literally no other teacher on the planet has.  

He saves the people on Earth who betrayed him, but also then turns away from them, and does not forgive them. But he's found his own place were he doesn't even need to consider them.

Where Grace ends up is a great plot twist - it's satisfying because it's in keeping with what we know about the character, and his motivations, and it takes him full circle back to a different version of his "happy place" where he was at the start of the movie. Which would not have happened if he had returned to Earth.
4. I agree, with the tone and conflict up to that point I knew exactly that the evolved predator in the xenonite containers really wouldn't amount to any real problem for Grace or Rocky. I think it could've ended in 10 other ways that would have worked as well or better. Leave him on Erid, have him return to Earth too late, have him not return at all, have him get to Rocky to find its too late etc. Not that a happy ending is bad but it was so expected and already kind of dragging I just wanted the credits to roll. I really liked the movie otherwise until the last 20 min. 
  - I see where you’re coming from. Normally I’d be bothered in the way you described, but I found myself completely fine rolling with the safe, Hollywood approach to the story. It knew what it was so I was like “eh whatever”
  - Also - it seemed to me that he had already made peace with not returning to earth which did seem to lessen the potential sacrifice a tiny bit
5. I agree with your point from a literary analysis sense, and I really liked the movie, but at no point was it aiming to be a thematic "masterpiece" imo. It is clear from the start that this is an action-comedy space blockbuster for the whole family. That tone would be sacrificed (no pun intended) if the movie truly decided to put the audience in Grace's shoes by formally demonstrating the true costs of bravery.

People keep asking me what I thought of the movie and my answer is "it was a great space blockbuster but not very good sci-fi." My bigger issue is that Rocky is basically just a human in rock form that speaks a different language. There's nothing remotely thoughtful about the ways in which such an alien species would be fundamentally different from us.

That’s not cinema as in ‘a work you sit with and think about,’ more like content engineered to survive distracted viewing.

If you grow up on movies that explain themselves out loud every ten minutes, anything more subtle; Mad Men, The Age of Innocence, Breaking Bad older slow burn noirs  is going to feel ‘boring’ or ‘confusing’ by comparison. So for me the problem isn’t just that Netflix isn’t theatrical, it’s that their house style (with some exceptions) actively trains people not to do the kind of focused watching or develop the comprehension skills that cinema needs.

## 5. People are too afraid to criticize slow pacing in movies because they associate slow movies with high art

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Movies can be criticized for all sorts of things, like screenplay, cinematography, acting, visual effects, sound, set design, and pacing. But when pacing is criticized, it’s usually about movies being too fast, not too slow. It’s surprisingly rare to see people openly criticize slow pacing. And when someone does say a movie is boring because it drags, they often get dismissed as having a short attention span and are told to go watch TikTok instead.

There’s definitely some truth behind that reaction. The rise of short form content has changed how people consume media. Platforms like TikTok constantly train users to expect fast stimulation and instant payoff. Over time, that can reduce patience for slower, more deliberate art forms. So in some cases, complaints about slow pacing really do come from an audience that has been conditioned to expect constant engagement.

But that’s only part of the picture. What’s interesting is how this has created a kind of cultural overcorrection. Among cinephiles, slow pacing has become associated with sophistication, artistic depth, and intellectual maturity. Enjoying slow films is often treated as a sign of good taste, while disliking them can be framed as a lack of intelligence or refinement. In other words, pacing is no longer judged purely on whether it serves the story, but also on what it signals about the viewer.

This is where the problem lies. Slow pacing is only effective if it enhances narrative or charactwrs. When slowness becomes indulgent, repetitive, or unnecessarily drawn out, it can absolutely weaken the film. And criticizing that isn’t a sign of low attention span, it’s a legitimate critique of craft.

Take 2001: A Space Odyssey as an example. It’s widely regarded as a masterpiece for its visuals, ambition, and philosophical themes. But it’s also a film where the pacing can feel excessively slow to many viewers. Long stretches with minimal dialogue and extended sequences can create a sense of awe, but they can also test the audience’s engagement. For me, it could have been my favorite film of all time if it was an hour shorter. Saying that the film could be tighter doesn’t mean you don’t get it, it simply means you’re evaluating how effectively the pacing serves the experience.

The discussion around pacing has become less about the film itself and more about identity. People defend slow movies not just because they enjoy them, but because those movies are tied to a certain image of taste and intellect. But if we strip that away, the standard should be simple: does the pacing make the movie better or worse? If it doesn’t, then it’s completely fair to criticize it, regardless of whether it’s slow or fast.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

None of us want to sound like *those* people, but... sometimes a movie actually is boring. And I say that as someone who likes a lot of slow, plodding "boring" movies. 
2. >It’s surprisingly rare to see people openly criticize slow pacing.

I think this is all dependent on what you're expecting to get out of a film. When I was a teenager, I was watching movies purely for popcorn entertainment purposes. I wanted to feel a specific simple emotion when I watched a movie, usually excitement. Now, I watch things like 2001 and see them more as "every frame's a painting" movies. The story doesn't need every frame to be a painting, but it's so much more interesting because of it. There are movies that I'd potentially be bored by because of the pacing, but I'm not because I'm enjoying the imagery as much as the story.
